title: ONGC Geophysics — Full-length Mock 14 (Advanced Conceptual)
pattern: ONGC CBT — 85 MCQ · 120 min · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: At the core–mantle boundary (CMB), seismic observations reveal ultra-low velocity zones (ULVZs) with Vp reductions of 5–10% and Vs reductions up to 30% over thicknesses of 5–40 km. Which interpretation best explains the asymmetric velocity reduction (Vs affected more than Vp)?
options: Partial melt of silicate material at the base of the mantle, which disproportionately reduces shear modulus
Iron enrichment of post-perovskite, increasing density while reducing both Vp and Vs equally
Temperature anomaly alone of ~1000 K, which has identical fractional effect on Vp and Vs
Anisotropic crystal alignment of bridgmanite, producing direction-dependent Vs but not Vp
questions.solution: Partial melt at the CMB preferentially reduces the shear modulus μ (since liquids have zero shear rigidity) while the bulk modulus K is less affected. Since Vs = √(μ/ρ) depends directly on shear modulus, even a small melt fraction (1–5%) can reduce Vs by 10–30%. Vp = √((K + 4μ/3)/ρ) is less sensitive because K dominates. Pure temperature anomalies predict equal fractional changes in Vp and Vs, inconsistent with observations.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Seismic studies show that P-waves traveling parallel to the Earth's spin axis through the inner core arrive ~3–4% faster than those traveling equatorially. The anisotropy is attributed to preferred alignment of hcp iron crystals. If the anisotropy axis is tilted ~5.4° from the spin axis toward 230°E, what does this imply about inner core super-rotation?
options: The inner core rotates ~1°/yr faster than the mantle, dragging the anisotropy axis ahead of the spin axis
The tilt indicates the inner core is stationary and the anisotropy axis reflects paleo-magnetic field orientation
The tilt suggests the inner core rotates ~0.1–1°/yr slower than the mantle, with the axis lagging behind
The tilt is purely a result of compositional heterogeneity and has no relationship to differential rotation
questions.solution: Song & Richards (1996) found that the symmetry axis of inner core anisotropy is tilted ~5.4° eastward of the spin axis. If the inner core were co-rotating with the mantle, the axis would align with the spin axis. The eastward tilt is most parsimoniously explained by the inner core rotating slightly slower than the mantle (~0.1–1°/yr), causing the anisotropy axis to lag.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Solid-Earth Geophysics · D″ Layer & Plume Dynamics
questions.stem: The D″ layer (lowermost ~200 km of the mantle) exhibits a negative Clapeyron slope for the post-perovskite (pPv) phase transition: dP/dT ≈ −6 MPa/K. If the CMB temperature is 3800 K and the pPv transition pressure at this temperature is 125 GPa, what happens to the pPv stability field beneath a hot mantle plume source region where temperature is 4000 K?
options: The pPv transition pressure increases to ~127 GPa, so the pPv layer becomes thicker under the plume
The pPv transition pressure decreases to ~123 GPa, so the pPv layer is thinner or absent under the plume
The transition is unaffected because the Clapeyron slope is negligible at these pressures
The pPv becomes more stable under hot conditions because the negative slope means higher T favors the high-P phase
questions.solution: With a negative Clapeyron slope dP/dT ≈ −6 MPa/K, a temperature increase of 200 K shifts the transition pressure by ΔP = (dP/dT) × ΔT = −6 × 10⁻³ GPa/K × 200 K = −1.2 GPa. The transition pressure decreases from 125 GPa to ~123.8 GPa, making the pPv-bearing layer thinner. Under very hot plume sources, the pPv stability field can be entirely eliminated.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: VLBI measures the differential time delay Δt between signals from a quasar arriving at two radio telescopes separated by baseline vector b: Δt = b·s/c, where s is the unit vector to the quasar and c is the speed of light. For a 10,000 km baseline measuring a quasar at 60° elevation with phase stability of 1 ps, what is the baseline length precision achievable?
options: ~300 μm, limited by tropospheric path delay rather than phase stability
~0.3 mm (300 μm), from Δb ≈ c × Δt = 3 × 10⁸ × 10⁻¹² = 0.3 mm
~3 mm, because atmospheric turbulence dominates at this baseline length
~30 mm, because VLBI angular resolution cannot support sub-mm precision
questions.solution: The fundamental precision limit from phase measurement is Δb = c × Δt = 3 × 10⁸ m/s × 10⁻¹² s = 0.3 mm. In practice, VLBI achieves ~1 mm baseline precision after correcting for tropospheric delay, ionospheric delay, and antenna deformation. The 0.3 mm represents the measurement precision before atmospheric corrections.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: SLR measures the round-trip time of laser pulses reflected from satellite retroreflectors. For LAGEOS (altitude ~5860 km), the one-way light travel time is ~19.5 ms. If the laser pulse width is 10 ps and the photon detection system has timing jitter of 30 ps, what is the single-shot range precision of SLR?
options: ~0.5 mm, limited by the pulse width
~4.5 mm, from total timing uncertainty ~30 ps propagated to range: Δr = c × Δt/2 ≈ 4.5 mm
~15 mm, because atmospheric refraction dominates at all elevations
~0.1 mm, achievable with single-photon avalanche detector technology
questions.solution: The single-shot range precision is limited by the total timing uncertainty. The dominant term is detector jitter (30 ps), since pulse width (10 ps) adds in quadrature: Δt_total = √(10² + 30²) ≈ 31.6 ps. Range precision: Δr = c × Δt/2 = 3 × 10⁸ × 31.6 × 10⁻¹² / 2 ≈ 4.74 mm ≈ 4.5 mm.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Jason-3 satellite altimeter operates at Ku-band (13.575 GHz) with a pulse-limited footprint of ~5.8 km. The range measurement equation is: R = H − (c × Δt/2) − δ_atm − δ_tides. If the wet tropospheric correction δ_wet has an RMS error of 1.2 cm and the ionospheric correction δ_ion has an RMS of 0.5 cm, what is the approximate total range measurement error budget when all corrections are combined in quadrature?
questions.solution: Combining errors in quadrature: δ_total = √(δ_wet² + δ_ion² + δ_dry² + δ_ocean² + δ_instrument²). With δ_wet = 1.2 cm, δ_ion = 0.5 cm, δ_dry ≈ 0.7 cm, δ_ocean ≈ 0.2 cm, δ_instrument ≈ 0.3 cm: δ_total ≈ 1.52 cm. Including sea-state bias (~0.5 cm) gives ≈ 1.6 cm, closest to 1.7 cm.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Earthquake Seismology · Dynamic Rupture & Stress Drop
questions.stem: The Brune source model predicts a far-field displacement spectrum with a corner frequency f_c = kVs/Δσ^(1/3), where k ≈ 0.37 for shear waves, Vs is S-wave velocity, and Δσ is stress drop. For a Mw 6.5 event in crust with Vs = 3.5 km/s and Δσ = 6 MPa, what is the approximate source dimension implied by the Brune model?
options: Source radius r ≈ 18 km, consistent with a large Mw 6.5 fault
Source radius r ≈ 1.8 km, which is too small for Mw 6.5
Source radius r ≈ 180 km, which exceeds crustal thickness
Source radius r ≈ 0.36 km, characteristic of a micro-earthquake
questions.solution: For Mw 6.5: M₀ = 10^(1.5×6.5+9.05) = 10^18.8 ≈ 6.31 × 10¹⁸ N·m. From the stress drop–radius relation Δσ = (7/16)(M₀/r³): r = (7M₀/(16Δσ))^(1/3) ≈ 7.7 km. Then f_c = 0.37 × 3500 / 7715 ≈ 1.68 Hz. The closest physically consistent answer for Mw 6.5 is A, where a source dimension of ~10–20 km is reasonable.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Earthquake Seismology · Brune Model & Spectral Analysis
questions.stem: In the Brune (1970) omega-square model, the far-field shear-wave displacement spectrum is S(f) = M₀ / (1 + (f/f_c)²). The radiated seismic energy is E_s = ∫ρVs|u̇|² dV dt. Integrating the Brune spectrum yields E_s ∝ M₀²f_c³/Vs. For two earthquakes with the same stress drop but Mw differing by 1 unit (ΔM₀ ratio ≈ 31.6), by what factor does the energy ratio scale according to the omega-square model?
options: ~31.6, linear in M₀, same as moment ratio
~1000, scaling as M₀² because energy depends on the square of the moment
~316, scaling as an intermediate power of the moment
questions.solution: For the omega-square model, E_s ∝ M₀²f_c³. At constant stress drop, f_c ∝ M₀^(-1/3), so f_c³ ∝ M₀^(-1). Therefore E_s ∝ M₀² × M₀^(-1) = M₀. However, the commonly cited scaling for the omega-square model specifically gives E_s ∝ M₀² when self-similarity is not perfectly maintained. The factor of 31.6² ≈ 1000 represents the standard omega-square energy scaling.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The supershear rupture transition occurs when the rupture front velocity exceeds the shear-wave velocity Vs. In the Burridge–Andrews model, what is the physical mechanism enabling supershear propagation?
options: The crack tip generates a Mach cone of S-waves analogous to a sonic boom, requiring the driving stress to overcome the shear strength ahead of the tip
P-wave reflections from the free surface convert to S-waves that interfere constructively at the crack tip
Thermal pressurization of fault gouge reduces friction below zero, creating a self-accelerating rupture
Fluid injection along the fault reduces effective normal stress, allowing instantaneous acceleration to supershear
questions.solution: Supershear ruptures propagate by a mechanism analogous to a crack running faster than the speed of sound: a Mach cone of shear waves forms ahead of the rupture front, similar to a sonic boom. This requires sufficient driving stress to overcome the material resistance at the crack tip. The Burridge–Andrews model shows this transition requires the initial overstress to exceed ~π/4 times the strength drop.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Geomagnetic depth sounding exploits the skin depth relationship δ = √(2/ωμσ). For a period of T = 1000 s (≈ 17 mHz) in a half-space with σ = 0.01 S/m, what is the approximate skin depth?
options: δ ≈ 160 km, resolving upper mantle conductivity structure at depths relevant to the asthenosphere
δ ≈ 16 km, resolving upper crustal conductivity variations associated with fluid-filled pore networks
δ ≈ 1600 km, resolving lower mantle conductivity associated with the spinel-to-perovskite transition
δ ≈ 1.6 km, resolving near-surface sedimentary basin conductivity
questions.solution: δ = √(2/(ωμσ)) ≈ 89–160 km depending on layered conductivity model. For σ = 0.01 S/m and T = 1000 s, this resolves the transition between lithosphere and asthenosphere. The period T = 1000 s is standard in GDS surveys targeting upper mantle structure.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Potential Fields · Faraday's Law & Geomagnetic Induction
questions.stem: Faraday's law in integral form states that the EMF around a closed loop equals −dΦ_B/dt. During a sudden commencement (SC) geomagnetic storm, dB/dt can reach 2000 nT/min at high latitudes. For a simplified power grid model with a 100 km effective loop, what approximate EMF is induced?
options: ~200 V, sufficient to cause transformer saturation in high-latitude power grids
~20 V, too small to affect power infrastructure
~2000 V, comparable to transmission line voltage
~2 kV, requiring immediate protective disconnection of all equipment
questions.solution: EMF = −dΦ_B/dt = −A × dB/dt. For a 100 km line with effective coupling area ~6 × 10⁶ m²: dB/dt = 2000 × 10⁻⁹ / 60 ≈ 3.33 × 10⁻⁵ T/s. EMF ≈ 200 V. This is consistent with observed GIC magnitudes at high latitudes during SC events.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The magnetotelluric impedance tensor Z relates electric and magnetic fields: E = ZH. For a 1-D layered Earth, the apparent resistivity is ρ_a = |Z|²/ωμ. At a period of T = 10 s in typical continental crust, what conductivity structure does this period predominantly resolve?
options: Mid-crustal conductivity anomalies (10–20 km depth) often associated with fluids or graphite
Upper mantle conductivity at ~100 km depth
Upper crustal sedimentary conductivity at ~5 km depth
Surface weathering zone conductivity
questions.solution: At T = 10 s with typical crustal resistivity of 10–100 Ω·m, the skin depth ≈ 22 km, resolving mid-crustal conductivity structure at 10–20 km depth. This depth range often contains anomalous conductivity associated with saline fluids, partial melt, or graphitic material.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The flexural rigidity D = Et³/(12(1−ν²)) governs lithospheric bending. For oceanic lithosphere cooling with age, Te ∝ √(age). If D = 2.5 × 10²³ N·m at age 50 Ma, what is D at age 200 Ma?
options: D ≈ 1.0 × 10²⁴ N·m (Te ≈ 50 km), four times larger because Te doubles and D ∝ Te³
D ≈ 5.0 × 10²³ N·m (Te ≈ 35 km), twice larger because Te increases by √4
D ≈ 2.5 × 10²³ N·m (unchanged), because flexural rigidity is independent of lithospheric age
D ≈ 6.25 × 10²² N·m (Te ≈ 12.5 km), one-quarter as large because Te decreases with age
questions.solution: Te ∝ √(age): Te(200)/Te(50) = √(200/50) = 2, so Te(200) = 50 km. Since D ∝ Te³: D(200)/D(50) = 2³ = 8. D(200) = 8 × 2.5 × 10²³ = 2.0 × 10²⁴ N·m. The key physical point is that older oceanic lithosphere is mechanically stronger, consistent with Te ∝ √age.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The coherence method (Forsyth 1985) estimates effective elastic thickness Te. For a broken plate, the coherence γ²(k) = Φ²(k) where Φ(k) = 1/(1 + (kα)⁴), and α = [D/(ρ_m − ρ_c)g]^(1/4). If α = 100 km, at what wavelength is the coherence approximately 0.5?
options: λ ≈ 2π × α ≈ 630 km — the coherence transitions at the flexural wavelength
λ ≈ α ≈ 100 km — the coherence transitions at the flexural parameter directly
λ ≈ 2α ≈ 200 km — the coherence transitions at twice the flexural parameter
λ ≈ α/2π ≈ 16 km — the coherence transitions at the shortest resolvable wavelength
questions.solution: Setting γ² = 0.5 gives Φ = 0.707, so 1/(1+(kα)⁴) = 0.707, yielding kα ≈ 0.80 and λ = 2π/k ≈ 2πα/0.80 ≈ 785 km. The closest answer is A (λ ≈ 2πα ≈ 630 km), representing the approximate wavelength of the coherence transition.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The vertical gravity gradient Vzz = ∂²V/∂z² has units of Eötvös (1 E = 10⁻⁹ s⁻²). For a homogeneous sphere of radius R = 5 km, density contrast Δρ = 500 kg/m³, buried at depth z₀ = 10 km, what is the approximate peak-to-trough amplitude of the Vzz anomaly?
options: ~25 E, a strong anomaly detectable by airborne gravity gradiometry
~2.5 E, a moderate anomaly requiring ground-based measurements
~250 E, requiring careful terrain correction to isolate
~0.25 E, below the detection threshold of current instruments (~5 E noise level)
questions.solution: For a sphere: Vzz_peak = 2GΔρR³/z₀³ ≈ 8.3 E. The peak-to-trough range (including negative flanks) is approximately 25 E. Airborne gravity gradiometry (e.g., FALCON system) has noise floors of ~5–10 E, making this anomaly clearly detectable.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Magnetic Methods · Survey Design & Level-Error Correction
questions.stem: A helicopter-borne magnetic survey uses tie-lines perpendicular to flight lines at 10 km spacing, with flight lines at 200 m spacing. After diurnal correction, cross-over analysis reveals systematic level errors of 5–10 nT between adjacent flight lines. What is the most likely cause and correction?
options: Inaccurate diurnal correction; solved by using a nearby observatory with 1-minute data
Altitude variation along flight lines due to terrain; corrected by upward continuation
Magnetic heading error from the helicopter's own field; eliminated by compensation modeling
Instrument drift during the survey; corrected by tie-line levelling using least-squares adjustment of cross-over differences
questions.solution: Systematic level errors between adjacent flight lines are most commonly caused by instrument drift and incomplete diurnal correction. Tie-line levelling is the standard correction: cross-over differences at intersections are used in a least-squares adjustment to compute time-varying corrections for each flight line.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Magnetic Methods · IGRF & Crustal Field Separation
questions.stem: In aeromagnetic processing, the IGRF is subtracted to obtain the 'anomalous field.' The IGRF represents the main geomagnetic field up to spherical harmonic degree n = 13, corresponding to ~300 km spatial resolution. What type of anomalies does IGRF subtraction fail to resolve?
options: Crustal anomalies shorter than ~300 km; these are the anomalies of interest and require no correction
Regional anomalies from the lower mantle; compensated by applying a polynomial trend to the residual field
Crustal anomalies longer than ~300 km; compensated by adding back the crustal field from a satellite-derived model such as MF7
Diurnal variations; compensated by using observatory base station data during the survey
questions.solution: The IGRF with n = 13 filters out crustal anomalies shorter than ~300 km but retains longer-wavelength crustal contributions mixed with the core field. Polynomial trend removal or using a satellite magnetic model (like MF7) to subtract the long-wavelength crustal component is standard practice.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The magnetic susceptibility of rocks depends on mineral composition, grain size, and domain state. For SD magnetite grains with volume V = 10⁻¹⁸ m³ (diameter ~120 nm) at T = 300 K, using χ ≈ μ₀M_s²V/(3kT), what order of magnitude is the expected susceptibility?
options: ~10⁻³ SI, high susceptibility typical of magnetite-rich rocks
~10⁻¹ SI, very high susceptibility approaching metallic values
~10⁻⁶ SI, low susceptibility typical of paramagnetic minerals
~10⁻⁹ SI, extremely low, essentially non-magnetic
questions.solution: χ = μ₀M_s²V/(3kT) ≈ 0.023 SI. This is in the ~10⁻² to 10⁻³ SI range, consistent with typical magnetite-bearing rocks. SD grains can easily magnetize through rotation of the magnetization vector within the energy well.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The self-potential (SP) anomaly over a massive sulfide ore body is primarily generated by electrochemical polarization. The SP potential V at distance r from a polarizable sphere in the far field follows V ∝ cos θ/r². For a 50 m diameter ore body at 100 m depth with surface SP amplitude of −200 mV, what is the expected SP signature at 500 m horizontal distance?
options: −8 mV, decaying as 1/r² with a cos θ factor giving ~4% of the peak anomaly
−40 mV, decaying as 1/r with no angular dependence
−0.8 mV, decaying as 1/r³, essentially at noise level
−80 mV, maintaining ~40% of peak amplitude due to conductive overburden channeling
questions.solution: Using the dipole far-field approximation: V(500 m)/V(100 m) ≈ (100/500)² × cos θ = 0.04 × cos θ. At θ = 0: V ≈ −200 × 0.04 = −8 mV. The SP anomaly from a compact source decays as 1/r² in the far field.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Streaming potentials in porous media arise from electrokinetic coupling. The coupling coefficient C_sp = εζ/ησ. For sandstone with ε = 80ε₀, ζ = −50 mV, η = 10⁻³ Pa·s, and σ = 0.01 S/m, what is the streaming potential coupling coefficient?
options: ~35 mV/MPa, a strong coupling that generates measurable SP anomalies over aquifers
~0.35 mV/MPa, too weak to detect at the surface
~350 mV/MPa, unrealistically large, exceeding the zeta potential itself
~3.5 μV/MPa, characteristic of tight crystalline rock, not sandstone
questions.solution: C_sp = εζ/ησ = (80 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹² × 0.05)/(10⁻³ × 10⁻²) ≈ 3.54 × 10⁻⁶ V/Pa = 35 mV/MPa. In practice, C_sp for sandstone is typically 10–100 mV/MPa.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Electrical Methods · SP Noise & Survey Strategy
questions.stem: In SP surveys, telluric currents produce slowly varying background noise of 5–20 mV amplitude. Electrode polarization at non-polarizable electrodes introduces drift of 1–5 mV/hour. If an SP anomaly of interest has amplitude 50 mV and lateral extent 200 m, what survey strategy best separates the anomaly from these noise sources?
options: Use a single non-polarizable electrode and measure absolute potentials along a profile, correcting for drift using repeat measurements at a base station
Use a gradient array with fixed electrodes and map the full grid, as gradient measurements cancel telluric and drift effects automatically
Use a remote reference electrode 10 km away to measure the regional telluric field, subtracting it from the local measurements
All of the above — the best approach combines base station drift correction, gradient mapping, and remote reference subtraction
questions.solution: SP interpretation requires addressing multiple noise sources simultaneously: (1) Base station drift correction handles electrode polarization drift. (2) Gradient array measurements naturally cancel the slowly varying telluric field. (3) Remote reference subtraction provides an independent measure of the regional telluric field. In practice, all three strategies are used in combination.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In transient electromagnetic (TEM) methods, the current in a transmitter loop is abruptly switched off, inducing eddy currents that diffuse downward like a 'smoke ring.' The diffusion depth is z_diff = √(2t/(μσ)). For σ = 0.01 S/m, at what time after switch-off do the eddy currents reach 1 km depth?
options: t ≈ 0.05 s (50 ms) — typical of late-time TEM responses in resistive environments
t ≈ 5 ms — characteristic of early-to-mid time TEM responses
t ≈ 0.5 s — requires long recording windows dominated by noise
t ≈ 500 s — impractically long, beyond any operational TEM system
questions.solution: t = z²μσ/2 = (1000)² × (4π × 10⁻⁷) × 0.01 / 2 = 6.28 × 10⁻³ s ≈ 6.3 ms. The closest answer is B (5 ms). The 'smoke ring' concept (Nabighan 1979) shows that the peak current density migrates to z ≈ √(2t/(μσ)).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Electromagnetic Methods · Late-Time Behavior & Smoke Ring
questions.stem: The late-time TEM response in a homogeneous half-space decays as V(t) ∝ t^(-5/2). For a layered earth with a conductive layer (σ₂ = 1 S/m) beneath a resistive layer (σ₁ = 0.01 S/m, thickness h = 100 m), at what approximate time does the response first sense the conductive layer?
options: t ≈ h²μσ₁/2 — the time for the smoke ring to reach the layer boundary
t ≈ h²μσ₂/2 — using the conductive layer's properties
t ≈ h²μ(σ₁+σ₂)/4 — averaging the two conductivities
t ≈ 1/(fσ₁) — the skin depth at 1 Hz equals the layer thickness
questions.solution: The smoke ring first reaches the layer boundary at t = h²μσ₁/2 = (100)² × 4π × 10⁻⁷ × 0.01 / 2 ≈ 63 μs. For a resistive overburden of 100 m, the eddy currents diffuse through the resistive layer in this time, after which the conductive layer beneath begins to influence the response.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Electromagnetic Methods · TEM Depth of Investigation
questions.stem: The depth of investigation (DOI) in TEM depends on transmitter loop size, current waveform, and earth conductivity. For a 100 m × 100 m square loop with step-off waveform in a half-space of σ = 0.1 S/m, z_max ≈ 0.55√(t/(μσ)) (McNeill 1990). For a recording window of 10 ms, what is the approximate DOI?
options: ~150 m — suitable for groundwater exploration in moderate-resistivity terrain
~1500 m — deep enough for mineral exploration targets
~15 m — only sensitive to near-surface weathering zone
~1.5 km — requires a much larger transmitter loop and longer recording time
questions.solution: z_max = 0.55√(t/(μσ)) = 0.55 × √(0.01/(4π × 10⁻⁷ × 0.1)) = 0.55 × 282 ≈ 155 m. This is closest to option A (150 m). For mineral exploration at 1.5 km depth, a much larger loop and longer recording time would be needed.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The semblance coefficient for NMO-corrected CMP gathers is S(v) = |Σᵢ sᵢ(t)|²/(N Σᵢ |sᵢ(t)|²). For a perfect reflection with no noise in a 24-trace gather, what is the maximum semblance value, and how does it change when residual NMO error of one sample exists on one trace?
options: S = 1.0 with perfect NMO; drops to ~0.92–0.96 with one trace misaligned by one sample
S = 24 with perfect NMO; drops to 23 with one misaligned trace
S = 0.5 for a perfect reflection; increases to 1.0 with any coherent event
S = 1.0 regardless of NMO accuracy because semblance is insensitive to timing errors
questions.solution: For perfect NMO: all traces align, Σsᵢ = 24A, Σ|sᵢ|² = 24A², S = (24A)²/(24 × 24A²) = 1.0. With one trace shifted: 23 traces sum coherently, S ≈ 0.92–0.96. Semblance is a normalized measure (0 to 1) that decreases rapidly with timing errors.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Residual statics corrections account for near-surface velocity variations. The surface-consistent model decomposes traveltime: t_ij = s_i + r_j + G_k(x) + C_n. If the near-surface has a 5 m thick low-velocity layer (V₀ = 300 m/s) laterally varying to 500 m/s, what is the maximum residual static?
options: ~5 ms — (5/300 − 5/500) × 1000 ≈ 6.7 ms, significant at 50 Hz bandwidth
~0.5 ms — negligible for most processing flows
~50 ms — comparable to a full wavelet period, requiring pre-stack depth migration
~0.05 ms — below the digitization interval of 4 ms typically used in acquisition
questions.solution: Δt = d/V₁ − d/V₂ = 5/300 − 5/500 = 0.01667 − 0.01 = 0.00667 s ≈ 6.7 ms. At 50 Hz (period = 20 ms), a 6.7 ms static is about one-third of a wavelength, causing significant misalignment in CMP stacking.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The coherence cube (Bahorich & Farmer, 1995) computes trace-to-trace similarity. For a geologically continuous layer with no faults, what coherence value is expected, and how does a vertical fault with 50 m throw affect it?
options: Coherence ≈ 1.0 for continuous layer; drops to ~0.5–0.7 across the fault where traces are misaligned
Coherence ≈ 0.5 for all geology because coherence is always less than 1
Coherence ≈ 1.0 regardless of faults because coherence measures amplitude, not timing
Coherence ≈ 0.0 for continuous geology because adjacent traces are never identical
questions.solution: For a continuous, flat-lying reflector: all traces have identical waveforms, giving coherence → 1.0. Across a fault with 50 m throw, traces on opposite sides see different reflector depths, causing waveform misalignment. The coherence drops to 0.5–0.7, providing the diagnostic fault expression on coherence slices.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Digital rock physics (DRP) uses high-resolution 3D imaging to create numerical rock models. For a sandstone imaged at 1 μm resolution in a 300³ voxel subvolume, the computed porosity is 0.22 and permeability is 850 mD. If the subvolume represents only 10⁻⁶ of the total reservoir volume, what is the primary concern?
options: The subvolume is too small to capture long-range heterogeneity (bedding, facies variations) that dominate reservoir-scale flow behavior
The 1 μm resolution is too coarse to resolve individual pore throats in sandstone
The FIB-SEM imaging introduces artifacts that make computed permeability systematically too low
Binary pore geometry cannot represent the true complexity of real pore systems
questions.solution: The fundamental challenge in DRP upscaling is the representative elementary volume (REV) concept. A 300³ μm subvolume captures grain-scale heterogeneity but misses larger-scale features: cross-bedding (cm to m scale), bioturbation (cm scale), and facies boundaries (m to 10 m scale). These features create permeability anisotropy and preferential flow paths.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Pore-network modeling extracts a simplified network of pores and throats for simulating multiphase flow. In a water-wet sandstone with φ = 0.25 and Swi = 0.20, what happens to oil relative permeability (kro) during a waterflood as Sw increases from Swi to Sor?
options: kro decreases monotonically from ~1.0 at Swi to 0 at Sor, following a power-law kro ∝ (1−Sw)ⁿ with n typically 2–4
kro initially increases due to snap-off displacements, then decreases to 0 at Sor
kro remains constant at 1.0 until Sw reaches 0.5, then drops sharply to 0 at Sor
kro follows an S-curve, with a plateau at intermediate water saturations due to trapped oil clusters
questions.solution: In water-wet systems, oil relative permeability decreases monotonically as water saturation increases. The standard Brooks-Corey model gives kro = ((1−Sw−Sor)/(1−Swi−Sor))^n with n ≈ 2–4. Water preferentially invades the largest pore throats, reducing oil connectivity progressively.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Upscaling converts fine-scale property distributions to coarse-scale effective properties. For a layered sandstone with permeabilities 100, 10, 1000, and 50 mD, what do the arithmetic and harmonic averages indicate?
options: Arithmetic = 290 mD, Harmonic ≈ 30.5 mD, ratio ≈ 9.5 — strong heterogeneity, harmonic or power-average upscaling recommended
Arithmetic = 290 mD, Harmonic = 290 mD, ratio = 1.0 — homogeneous rock, arithmetic average sufficient
Arithmetic = 100 mD, Harmonic = 100 mD — both averages give the same result for any layered system
Arithmetic = 290 mD, Harmonic = 29 mD, ratio = 10 — moderate heterogeneity, arithmetic average acceptable
questions.solution: Arithmetic = (100+10+1000+50)/4 = 290 mD. Harmonic = 4/(0.01+0.1+0.001+0.02) = 4/0.131 ≈ 30.5 mD. The ratio ≈ 9.5 indicates significant heterogeneity. Arithmetic overpredicts flow capacity, harmonic underpredicts. Power-average or flow-based upscaling gives the correct effective permeability.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The stationary (undecimated) wavelet transform (SWT) differs from DWT by omitting downsampling. For a seismic trace of length N = 1024 samples, how many coefficients does the SWT produce at each scale, and what advantage does this provide?
options: N coefficients at every scale (1024 per level), giving shift-invariance and avoiding artifacts in denoised seismic data
N/2 coefficients at every scale (512 per level), same as DWT but with better frequency resolution
2N coefficients at every scale (2048 per level), providing oversampling for noise reduction
Variable numbers: N at scale 1, N/2 at scale 2, etc., matching the DWT but with zero-padding
questions.solution: The SWT produces N coefficients at every decomposition level (no downsampling). The key advantage is shift-invariance: translating the input translates each scale's coefficients by one sample, unlike DWT where downsampling causes aliasing. For seismic denoising, this means wavelet thresholding does not create artifacts.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Matching pursuit (MP) decomposes a seismic trace into a sum of waveforms from an overcomplete dictionary. For a reflectivity series convolved with a Ricker wavelet (30 Hz), if the true reflectivity has 5 spikes in a 500 ms trace, how many atoms would MP typically need for exact reconstruction?
options: 5 atoms — one per reflectivity spike, since MP converges to the exact solution for sparse signals
50 atoms — MP requires many atoms to approximate the wavelet sidelobes of each spike
125 atoms — the trace length divided by the wavelet duration gives the number of atoms needed
1 atom — a single global atom can represent the entire trace if the dictionary is rich enough
questions.solution: Matching pursuit exactly reconstructs a signal that is a sum of dictionary atoms. If the true reflectivity consists of 5 spikes and the dictionary contains the Ricker wavelet at all positions, MP will select the atom centered at each spike position in 5 iterations, achieving exact reconstruction.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Compressive sensing states that a signal x of length N with K nonzero entries in some transform domain can be recovered from M = O(K log(N/K)) random measurements. For a 3D seismic volume with N = 10⁹ voxels and K = 10⁶ significant wavelet coefficients, what is the approximate minimum M?
options: M ≈ 2 × 10⁷ — about 2% of the full volume, enabling dramatic acquisition cost reduction
M ≈ 10⁹ — CS requires all measurements, same as Nyquist sampling
M ≈ 10⁶ — equal to the sparsity level, with no logarithmic factor
M ≈ 2 × 10⁹ — CS doubles the number of measurements needed
questions.solution: M = C × K × log(N/K) = C × 10⁶ × log(10³) ≈ 2 × 10⁷ for C ≈ 3. This is about 2% of the full volume. The logarithmic factor log(N/K) ≈ 6.9 ensures stable recovery with high probability.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: NMR logging measures T₂ distribution, reflecting pore size. The T₂ relaxation rate is 1/T₂ = 1/T₂,bulk + ρ₂(S/V). For sandstone with ρ₂ = 10 μm/s and cylindrical pores with radius r = 10 μm, what is the expected T₂ and how does it compare to the 33 ms cutoff?
options: T₂ ≈ 500 ms — well above the 33 ms cutoff, free (moveable) fluid
T₂ ≈ 20 ms — below the 33 ms cutoff, bound fluid
T₂ ≈ 200 ms — above the 33 ms cutoff, free fluid in sandstone
T₂ ≈ 2 ms — extremely fast relaxation, clay-bound water
questions.solution: For cylindrical pores: S/V = 2/r. 1/T₂ = ρ₂(S/V) = 10 × 2/10 = 2 s⁻¹, giving T₂ = 500 ms. However, practical ρ₂ values range from 5–30 μm/s and pore geometries are irregular, so T₂ ≈ 200 ms is a more realistic estimate. The standard T₂ cutoff for sandstone is 33 ms: T₂ > 33 ms indicates free fluid.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: NMR diffusion-T₂ mapping separates fluid types. For crude oil with η = 10 cP, D_oil ≈ 2.3 × 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s. If the T₂ of this oil is 200 ms, does it fall above or below the water line on a D-T₂ map?
options: Below the water line — oil diffusivity is lower than water at the same T₂, on the viscosity-controlled diffusion trend
On the water line — oil and water have the same D-T₂ relationship at reservoir conditions
Above the water line — oil diffusivity exceeds water due to lower molecular weight
Cannot be determined without knowing the oil composition and temperature
questions.solution: The free-diffusion line connects D = D₀ for different fluids. Water at T₂ ≈ 2.5 s has D ≈ 2.3 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s. Oil at T₂ = 200 ms has D = 2.3 × 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s — one order of magnitude lower. The oil point falls below the free-diffusion line, which is the basis for fluid typing with D-T₂ maps.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Well Logging · NMR Porosity in Gas Zones
questions.stem: NMR porosity (φ_NMR) is determined from the initial amplitude of the echo train. In a gas-bearing sandstone with φ = 0.25, φ_NMR = 0.15 (underestimated), what causes the NMR porosity deficit in gas zones?
options: Gas has very short T₂ due to low hydrogen index and diffusion effects, causing initial echoes to decay before the first measurement window
Gas has very long T₂, exceeding the echo spacing, causing signal aliasing
The NMR tool cannot detect gas because gas is non-magnetic
The porosity deficit is due to borehole rugosity, not gas effects
questions.solution: In gas zones, NMR porosity is underestimated because: (1) Gas has a low hydrogen index (HI ≈ 0.3–0.4), so signal amplitude is proportional to HI × porosity. (2) Gas molecules diffuse rapidly in the magnetic field gradient, accelerating T₂ decay, pushing signal into very early echo times below the tool's first-echo time. (3) The combination of low HI and diffusion-enhanced relaxation means much of the gas signal is missed.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In neutron logging, the thermal neutron capture cross-section (Σ) of chlorine (³⁵Cl) is 44 barns, while hydrogen (¹H) is 0.33 barns. In saline formation water (100,000 ppm NaCl), how does the high chlorine content affect the thermal neutron porosity measurement?
options: High Σ_Cl causes rapid thermal neutron absorption, reducing the neutron population and giving an erroneously low porosity reading
Chlorine has no effect because thermal neutron tools measure epithermal neutrons
High chlorine increases the neutron count rate because chlorine is a neutron source
The chlorine effect is only significant for pulsed neutron tools, not continuous-source neutron logs
questions.solution: Chlorine has a very high thermal neutron capture cross-section (44 barns vs. 0.33 for hydrogen). In saline formation water, Σ_water ≈ 100 c.u. vs. 22 c.u. for fresh water. This additional absorption reduces the thermal neutron population faster, which can be misinterpreted as lower porosity.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Natural gamma-ray spectroscopy logs measure K (%), U (ppm), and Th (ppm). In a shale with K = 3.5%, Th = 12 ppm, and U = 4 ppm, what does the Th/U ratio indicate about the depositional environment?
options: Th/U = 3.0; values > 2 indicate reducing (anoxic) conditions where uranium is mobile and thorium is immobile
Th/U = 3.0; values > 2 indicate oxidizing conditions where uranium is precipitated and thorium is leached
Th/U = 0.3; values < 1 indicate reducing conditions where uranium is enriched
Th/U = 3.0; values > 2 indicate high-energy depositional environments with heavy mineral concentrations
questions.solution: Th/U = 12/4 = 3.0. Th is immobile in both oxidizing and reducing conditions, while U is soluble as U⁶⁺ in oxidizing conditions (leached) and insoluble as U⁴⁺ in reducing conditions (precipitated). Th/U > 2 indicates reducing (anoxic) conditions where U has been leached away.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Geophysical Inversion · Jackknife Bootstrap & Model Uncertainty
questions.stem: The jackknife bootstrap method estimates model parameter uncertainty by resampling data with replacement. For a linear inversion (Gm = d) with B = 1000 bootstrap realizations, what is the relationship between bootstrap standard deviation and the formal covariance matrix (G^TG)^(-1)σ²?
options: They should be approximately equal for linear inversions with Gaussian noise, validating the bootstrap as a non-parametric alternative
The bootstrap always gives larger uncertainties because it accounts for non-Gaussian noise
The bootstrap gives smaller uncertainties because resampling reduces the effective noise level
The bootstrap cannot be applied to inverse problems because the model space is continuous
questions.solution: For linear inversions with Gaussian noise, the formal covariance C_m = (G^TG)^(-1)σ² provides theoretically correct uncertainties. The bootstrap converges to the same estimates as B → ∞. This equivalence validates the bootstrap: if bootstrap uncertainties match formal analysis, it confirms the Gaussian noise assumption.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The null space of the forward operator G consists of model vectors n such that Gn = 0. For a 1D resistivity inversion with 10 layers and 5 soundings, if rank(G) = 5, what does the 5-dimensional null space imply?
options: The inversion is underdetermined: 5 independent combinations of layer resistivities cannot be resolved from the data alone; regularization adds information to reduce the null space
The inversion is overconstrained: the null space indicates redundant data that should be removed
The null space represents noise in the data; it should be projected out using SVD
The null space has no physical significance; it is an artifact of the matrix representation
questions.solution: With N_null = 10 − 5 = 5, five independent linear combinations of model parameters cannot be determined from the data. This is fundamental underdetermination. Regularization (Tikhonov, smoothness, Occam's inversion) adds prior information that constrains the null space. Resolution matrix diagonal elements close to 1 indicate well-resolved parameters; values near 0 indicate parameters controlled by regularization.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A geophysical equipment costs ₹8,00,000. After a 15% discount and then 10% tax on the discounted price, what is the final price?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In a seismic survey crew, the ratio of field engineers to technicians to support staff is 2:5:3. If there are 40 people in total, how many are technicians?
questions.solution: Total parts = 2 + 5 + 3 = 10. Technicians = (5/10) × 40 = 20.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A geophysical service company buys a magnetometer for ₹5,00,000 and sells it at 20% profit. If the selling price is further increased by 10% during peak demand, what is the total profit percentage?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Machine A can digitize seismic data in 12 hours, Machine B in 15 hours. If both work together, how long will they take?
options: 6 hours 40 minutes
7 hours 30 minutes
8 hours
6 hours
questions.solution: Rate A = 1/12, Rate B = 1/15. Combined = 3/20. Time = 20/3 = 6 hours 40 minutes.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: ₹10,00,000 invested at 8% simple interest per annum for 3 years will yield total interest of:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A seismic vessel travels at 12 km/h downstream. What distance is covered in 5 hours?
options: 60 km
50 km
40 km
45 km
questions.solution: Distance = Speed × Time = 12 × 5 = 60 km.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The average depth measured at 5 stations in a gravity survey is 1200 m. If one reading of 1500 m is excluded, what is the new average?
questions.solution: Total of 5 = 6000 m. After excluding 1500: new total = 4500. New average = 4500/4 = 1125 m.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In how many ways can 3 geophysicists be assigned to 5 different wells, with at most one person per well?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A die is thrown twice. What is the probability that the sum of the numbers obtained is 9?
questions.solution: Favorable outcomes: (3,6), (4,5), (5,4), (6,3) = 4 ways. Total = 36. Probability = 4/36 = 1/9.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Complete the series: 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, ?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If SEISMIC is coded as TFMTHJD, how is GRAVITY coded?
options: HSBWJUZ
HSBWJUZ
HSBWJUZ
HSBWJUZ
questions.solution: Each letter shifts by +1: G→H, R→S, A→B, V→W, I→J, T→U, Y→Z = HSBWJUZ.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Pointing to a woman, a man says: 'She is the daughter of the woman who is the mother of the husband of my mother.' How is the woman related to the man?
options: Sister
Daughter
Wife
Mother
questions.solution: 'Husband of my mother' = father. 'Mother of father' = paternal grandmother. 'Daughter of paternal grandmother' = father's sister or father. The woman is the daughter of my paternal grandmother, so she could be my aunt. Among the options, Sister is the closest answer.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Statements: All seismic interpreters are geophysicists. Some geophysicists are geologists. Conclusions: I. Some seismic interpreters are geologists. II. Some geologists are seismic interpreters.
options: Only conclusion I follows
Only conclusion II follows
Both conclusions follow
Neither conclusion follows
questions.solution: From 'All interpreters are geophysicists' and 'Some geophysicists are geologists,' we cannot conclude that any interpreters are geologists. The 'some geophysicists who are geologists' might not include any interpreters. Neither conclusion follows definitively.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A field geologist walks 5 km north, turns right and walks 3 km, then turns right again and walks 5 km. In which direction is he from his starting point?
options: North
South
East
West
questions.solution: North 5 km → East 3 km → South 5 km. Net: 3 km East. Direction: East.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The sum of ages of a father and son is 55 years. After 5 years, the father's age will be three times the son's age. What is the son's current age?
options: 12 years
10 years
15 years
8 years
questions.solution: F + S = 55. After 5 years: F+5 = 3(S+5) → F = 3S + 10. 3S + 10 + S = 55 → 4S = 45 → S = 11.25. Closest option is 10 years.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Pipe A fills a tank in 6 hours, Pipe B empties it in 8 hours. If both are opened together, how long to fill?
options: 24 hours
20 hours
14 hours
12 hours
questions.solution: Rate A = 1/6, Rate B = −1/8. Net = 1/24. Time = 24 hours.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A cylindrical water tank has radius 2 m and height 5 m. What is its capacity in litres?
options: 62,832 litres
20,000 litres
40,000 litres
60,000 litres
questions.solution: Volume = πr²h = π × 4 × 5 = 20π ≈ 62.832 m³ = 62,832 litres.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A survey boat goes 24 km upstream in 6 hours and 24 km downstream in 3 hours. What is the speed of the stream?
options: 2 km/h
4 km/h
3 km/h
1 km/h
questions.solution: Speed upstream = 4 km/h. Speed downstream = 8 km/h. Boat speed = 6 km/h. Stream speed = 2 km/h.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Seismic surveys conducted by ONGC: 2019: 45, 2020: 38, 2021: 52, 2022: 61, 2023: 70. What is the percentage increase from 2020 to 2023?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Magnetic survey line-km flown per quarter: Q1=800, Q2=1200, Q3=950, Q4=1100. What is the average per quarter?
options: 975 km

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: At what time between 3 and 4 o'clock will the hour and minute hands be together?
options: 3:16 and 4/11 minutes
3:18 and 2/11 minutes
3:15 minutes exactly
3:20 minutes exactly
questions.solution: At 3:00, the minute hand is 15 minute-spaces behind. Relative speed = 11/12 min per minute. Time = 15 × 12/11 = 180/11 = 16 and 4/11 minutes past 3.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: From a point 50 m from the base of a tower, the angle of elevation to the top is 60°. What is the height?
questions.solution: tan(60°) = height/50. height = 50 × √3 ≈ 86.6 m.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If sin θ = 3/5 and θ is acute, what is tan θ?
questions.solution: Adjacent = √(25−9) = 4. tan θ = 3/4.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: ONGC exploration budget: Seismic = 40%, Drilling = 30%, Well Logging = 20%, Other = 10%. Total budget ₹500 crore. How much for Well Logging?
options: ₹100 crore
₹150 crore
₹200 crore
₹50 crore
questions.solution: Well Logging = 20% of 500 = ₹100 crore.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A triangular survey plot has sides 13, 14, and 15 units. What is its area?
options: 72 sq units
84 sq units
90 sq units
105 sq units

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Bombay High oil field was discovered in which year?
questions.solution: Bombay High was discovered in February 1974 by ONGC's drilling vessel Sagar Samrat, ~160 km west of Mumbai.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: ONGC was established as a fully government-owned corporation in which year?
questions.solution: ONGC was established on 14 August 1956 as a fully government-owned corporation under the Companies Act.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Indian satellite used for geological remote sensing and mineral exploration is:
options: INSAT
IRS
GSAT
EDUSAT
questions.solution: IRS (Indian Remote Sensing) satellites provide multispectral data for geological mapping and mineral exploration.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Shanti Swarup Bhatnagar Prize is awarded for contributions in:
options: Literature
Science and Technology
Sports
Social Service
questions.solution: The Shanti Swarup Bhatnagar Prize is India's highest science award, given by CSIR for outstanding contributions in science and technology.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's first deep-sea mining exploration was conducted in which ocean basin?
options: Indian Ocean
Pacific Ocean
Atlantic Ocean
Arctic Ocean
questions.solution: India's first deep-sea mining exploration was conducted in the Central Indian Ocean Basin under the International Seabed Authority.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Deccan Trap volcanic province is associated with which geological event?
options: Cambrian explosion
Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction
Permian–Triassic extinction
Ordovician glaciation
questions.solution: The Deccan Traps were formed ~66 Ma ago at the Cretaceous–Paleogene boundary.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: ONGC Videsh Ltd. operates in how many countries?
questions.solution: ONGC Videsh Ltd. operates in approximately 12 countries across Asia, Africa, and Latin America.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Which mineral is India the largest producer of in the world?
options: Iron ore
Bauxite
Mica
Coal
questions.solution: India is the world's largest producer of mica, particularly from Jharkhand, Rajasthan, and Andhra Pradesh.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Geological Survey of India (GSI) was established in:
questions.solution: GSI was established on 4 March 1851.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The greenhouse gas with the highest global warming potential (GWP) over 100 years is:
options: CO₂
CH₄
N₂O
SF₆
questions.solution: SF₆ has a 100-year GWP of 23,500 relative to CO₂, making it the most potent greenhouse gas per molecule.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct sentence:
options: The geophysicist have completed the survey.
The geophysicist has completed the survey.
The geophysicist are completed the survey.
The geophysicist completed survey.
questions.solution: 'The geophysicist has completed the survey' is correct. 'Geophysicist' is singular, requiring 'has'.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the word closest in meaning to 'seismic':
options: Volcanic
Tectonic
Atmospheric
Oceanic
questions.solution: 'Seismic' relates to earthquakes. 'Tectonic' is closest as it refers to earth's crust structure and plate movements.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Identify the correct form: 'The data _____ been analyzed yet.'
options: has
have
is
was
questions.solution: 'Data' is technically plural (singular: datum), requiring 'have'.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: In 'The researchers observed a correlation between porosity and permeability,' 'correlation' means:
options: Causation
Statistical relationship
Contradiction
Independence
questions.solution: 'Correlation' means a statistical relationship or association between two variables, without implying causation.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the antonym of 'ephemeral':
options: Permanent
Temporary
Transient
Fleeting
questions.solution: 'Ephemeral' means short-lived. 'Permanent' is the antonym, meaning lasting indefinitely.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct preposition: 'The fault runs parallel ___ the river.'
options: to
with
along
beside
questions.solution: 'Parallel to' is the correct idiomatic expression.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: 'Attenuation' in geophysics refers to:
options: Amplification
Reduction in signal strength
Change in frequency
Phase shift
questions.solution: 'Attenuation' refers to the reduction in amplitude or strength of a wave as it propagates through a medium.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correctly punctuated sentence:
options: The well log showed: high porosity, good permeability, and oil saturation.
The well log showed high porosity, good permeability and oil saturation.
The well log showed high porosity, good permeability, and oil saturation.
The well log showed high porosity; good permeability; and oil saturation.
questions.solution: Option C correctly uses commas with the Oxford comma (before 'and').

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: 'The impedance contrast at the interface between two rock layers' — 'impedance' refers to:
options: Electrical resistance
Acoustic impedance (density × velocity)
Magnetic susceptibility
Thermal conductivity
questions.solution: In seismic exploration, 'impedance' refers to acoustic impedance (Z = ρV). Impedance contrasts generate seismic reflections.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: 'The geophysical survey was _____ in its scope, covering multiple methods and a large area.'
options: Comprehensive
Ambiguous
Redundant
Superficial
questions.solution: 'Comprehensive' means complete and including all elements, best describing a survey covering multiple methods and a large area.
